title: Plain water — the default
bullets: Plain water is the foundation of any fasting protocol.
If still water feels flat, unflavored sparkling water is widely tolerated.

medicalNote.text: Adequate total-water intake for adults is roughly 2.7 L/day (women) and 3.7 L/day (men), including water from food. Hot weather and exercise increase the need.

title: Electrolytes (with care)
bullets: Some people add a small pinch of salt to water if they feel lightheaded.
Sweetened sports drinks have calories and sugar — they break a strict fast.

medicalNote.text: Prolonged water-only fasting (>24h) increases urinary sodium loss; a small amount of salt can mitigate lightheadedness for some people. Anyone on blood-pressure medication should consult a clinician first.
medicalNote.source: Cleveland Clinic / NIH NIDDK general nutrition guidance

title: Black coffee or plain tea
bullets: Plain coffee and unsweetened tea have negligible calories.
If you're unsure what 'still fasting' means for your goals, water-only is the strictest line.

medicalNote.text: EFSA considers up to 400 mg of caffeine per day (≈4 standard cups of coffee) safe for healthy non-pregnant adults. Caffeine acts as a mild appetite suppressant, which may help during longer fasting windows.

title: When to pause the fast
bullets: If you feel faint, confused, or genuinely unwell, end the fast and eat something gentle.
Discomfort isn't a badge — safety first.

medicalNote.text: Intermittent fasting is not appropriate for people who are pregnant or breastfeeding, have a history of eating disorders, are under 18, or take medication where missing meals affects dosing (e.g. insulin).
medicalNote.source: NIH NIA — Calorie Restriction and Fasting Diets

title: Bone or vegetable broth
bullets: Warm, lightly-salted broth is one of the gentlest ways to break a fast.
Use vegetable broth for vegan/vegetarian; bone broth adds collagen and minerals.

medicalNote.text: Gentle, liquid-based first meals reduce GI distress after a long fast compared with heavy, fatty meals — easier digestion is the goal during the first 30–60 minutes.
medicalNote.source: Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics — general refeeding guidance

title: Greek yogurt with berries
bullets: Plain Greek yogurt offers high protein density with live cultures.
Berries add fiber and antioxidants while keeping the sugar load modest.

medicalNote.text: A ¾-cup serving of Greek yogurt typically provides 15–20 g of protein, supporting satiety and muscle-protein synthesis at the first meal of the day.

title: Steel-cut oats with banana
bullets: Oats provide beta-glucan, a soluble fiber that slows glucose absorption.
Banana adds quick energy and potassium.

medicalNote.text: Beta-glucan in oats (≥3 g/day) is associated with lower LDL cholesterol — an established FDA-authorized health claim on oat-based foods.

title: Two-egg breakfast with avocado
bullets: Eggs deliver complete protein in a compact, easy-to-tolerate portion.
Avocado adds monounsaturated fat for steadier satiety.

medicalNote.text: Two large eggs supply about 12 g of complete protein with all nine essential amino acids — useful when breaking a fast on a smaller portion.

title: Miso soup with tofu
bullets: A warm, brothy bowl with fermented miso is gentle and easy to digest.
Silken tofu adds plant protein without a heavy texture.

medicalNote.text: Fermented foods like miso are associated with improved gut microbial diversity in controlled human trials — a small but consistent benefit at modest intake.

title: Fruit-and-nut starter plate
bullets: When appetite is small after a long fast, a curated plate is easier than a meal.
Pair sweet fruit with fat or protein to slow the glucose rise.

medicalNote.text: Pairing a carbohydrate source with protein or fat blunts the post-meal glucose spike — a useful pattern when easing off a long fasting window.
medicalNote.source: AHA Scientific Statement on glycemic index (Circulation 2002)

title: Pan-seared salmon (gentle portion)
bullets: Salmon at modest portion size delivers protein and omega-3 EPA/DHA.
Pair with cooked greens to keep the meal light.

medicalNote.text: The American Heart Association recommends two servings (~85 g cooked each) of oily fish per week for cardiovascular benefit from EPA/DHA omega-3s.
medicalNote.source: AHA Dietary Recommendation for Fish (2018 update)

title: Tofu scramble with greens
bullets: Crumbled firm tofu mimics scrambled eggs and absorbs seasoning well.
Turmeric adds color; nutritional yeast brings a savory, cheesy note.

medicalNote.text: Combining plant proteins across the day provides every essential amino acid — strict 'protein complementing' within a single meal is not required for adults.
medicalNote.source: Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics Position Paper on Vegetarian Diets (J Acad Nutr Diet 2016)

title: Lentil bowl with roasted vegetables
bullets: Lentils are a dense plant-protein and fiber source.
Roasted vegetables add volume, micronutrients, and satisfying texture.

medicalNote.text: One cup of cooked lentils provides about 18 g of protein and 16 g of fiber — among the densest plant-protein options available.

title: Quinoa and chickpea salad
bullets: Quinoa is a complete plant protein when you want a grain bowl base.
Chickpeas add staying power — the combination keeps you full for hours.

medicalNote.text: Quinoa is one of the few plant foods classified as a complete protein, containing all nine essential amino acids in meaningful amounts.

title: Grilled chicken with quinoa and greens
bullets: Lean grilled chicken delivers about 25–30 g protein in a compact portion.
Quinoa and greens complete the plate without crowding it.

medicalNote.text: The Recommended Dietary Allowance for protein in healthy adults is 0.8 g/kg/day; resistance-training or older adults may benefit from 1.2–1.6 g/kg/day, distributed across meals.

title: Salmon poke bowl
bullets: Salmon delivers protein plus EPA/DHA omega-3s in one main.
Rice, vegetables, and seaweed round out the bowl.

medicalNote.text: Two servings of oily fish per week (≈250 mg/day of EPA+DHA) is associated with reduced cardiovascular events in observational and trial evidence.

title: Tempeh stir-fry with brown rice
bullets: Tempeh is fermented soy with a firmer texture and slightly higher protein than tofu.
Stir-frying preserves vegetable crunch and keeps prep under 20 minutes.

medicalNote.text: Tempeh provides about 15–20 g of protein per 100 g and is fermented, which improves digestibility of soy isoflavones compared with whole soybeans.
medicalNote.source: Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics — Plant-Based Diets

title: Mediterranean white-bean plate
bullets: White beans deliver fiber and slow-burning carbohydrate.
Olive oil, tomato, and herbs anchor a Mediterranean profile.

ingredients.name: Cooked cannellini or great northern beans

medicalNote.text: Mediterranean dietary patterns are consistently associated with reduced cardiovascular and all-cause mortality across large prospective cohorts (PREDIMED trial; NEJM 2013).

title: Beef-and-vegetable bowl
bullets: Grass-fed lean beef is a dense iron and B12 source.
Pair with non-starchy vegetables to balance the plate.

medicalNote.text: Beef provides heme iron, which is absorbed more efficiently than non-heme iron from plants — a useful consideration for menstruating adults or known iron-deficiency.
medicalNote.source: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Iron Fact Sheet

title: Cottage cheese with cucumber and seeds
bullets: Cottage cheese is one of the densest dairy proteins.
Cucumber and seeds add crunch and minerals without big calories.

medicalNote.text: One cup of low-fat cottage cheese provides about 28 g of protein — comparable to a 4-oz serving of chicken breast.

title: Whole-grain pasta with chickpeas
bullets: Whole-grain pasta has more fiber than refined pasta.
Adding chickpeas turns a starch plate into a fiber + protein plate.

medicalNote.text: USDA Dietary Guidelines recommend making at least half of all grain servings whole grains, which are associated with reduced risk of cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes.

title: Snack: nut butter on apple
bullets: Mini snacks beat going 'zero to feast' if your energy crashes mid-window.
Pair carbs with fat for steadier fullness.

ingredients.name: Almond or peanut butter (no sugar added)

medicalNote.text: Apples (with skin) are a leading source of soluble pectin fiber and quercetin — both implicated in steadier post-meal glucose curves.
medicalNote.source: USDA FoodData Central + AHA dietary fiber guidance

title: Hard-boiled eggs + veggie sticks
bullets: Eggs ahead of time turn into instant protein later.
Carrot, celery, and bell pepper sticks make a fast snack plate.

medicalNote.text: Boiling a batch of eggs once a week is a simple meal-prep strategy: each large egg keeps roughly one week refrigerated and supplies 6 g of complete protein.

title: Tuna salad on greens
bullets: Canned tuna in water keeps lean protein in your pantry indefinitely.
Greek yogurt or olive oil dressings sit lighter than mayo.

medicalNote.text: Light/skipjack canned tuna averages about 25 g of protein per 100 g and is among the lowest-mercury commonly-available tuna varieties — the FDA categorizes it as a 'best choice' for most adults.
medicalNote.source: FDA / EPA Advice About Eating Fish (2021)

title: Black-bean and corn tacos
bullets: Beans + corn together form a complete-amino-acid profile (a classic complementing pair).
Wrapping in corn tortillas keeps the plate naturally gluten-free if needed.

medicalNote.text: Mexican beans-and-corn dishes are a long-standing example of cultural protein complementing — lysine in beans pairs with methionine in corn for a complete amino-acid profile.
medicalNote.source: FAO/WHO/UNU Expert Consultation on Protein Quality
